1: A new divine speech unit (vv1-4) legislating the exclusion of the ritually impure from the camp. The placement immediately after the census and Levitical organization chapters (1-4) makes sense: having established who is in the camp and how it is organized, the text now addresses who must be excluded. The holiness of the camp (where YHWH dwells, v3) requires the removal of severe ritual impurity.
2: Three categories of exclusion: (1) מְצֹרָע (məṣōrāʿ) — person with a skin disease; Leviticus 13-14 defines this category and requires exclusion (bāḏāḏ yēšēḇ — he shall live alone, Lev 13:46); (2) זָב (zāḇ) — person with a bodily discharge (Lev 15); (3) טָמֵא לָנֶפֶשׁ (ṭāmēʾ lānep̄eš) — impure through corpse contact (Num 19). Together these three cover the major categories of severe ritual impurity under the Levitical system.
3: ûmizzāḵār ʿaḏ-nəqēḇâ tənašṣēʾû — send them out, both male and female. The inclusion of women alongside men in the expulsion law establishes that ritual impurity is not a gendered category; the camp's holiness applies equally to all. The explicit reason: wəlōʾ yəṭamməʾû ʾet-maḥănêhem ʾăšer ʾănî šōkēn bətôkām — that they may not defile the camp where I dwell. YHWH's presence makes the camp a sacred space; its maintenance as such is the rationale for every purity law.
4: Israel's compliance: wayyaʿăśû-kēn bənê yiśrāʾēl wayyəšallĕḥû ʾôtām ʾel-miḥûṣ lammaḥăneh — they put them outside the camp, just as YHWH had spoken. The compliance formula closes the brief purity-exclusion unit. In context, this expulsion is temporary — the impure persons are quarantined until purification, not permanently excluded. The wilderness camp maintained a graduated perimeter: the most holy at center, the Levites surrounding, then all Israel, with the impure outside the boundary altogether.
5: A second divine speech unit (vv5-10) addresses unconfessed interpersonal wrongs. The new speech formula (waydabber YHWH ʾel-Moshe lēmôr) marks a distinct legal section covering restitution. The context — immediately after the purity laws — reflects a broad understanding of what constitutes moral pollution in the camp community.
6: The principle: when a man or woman commits any human sin (kol-ḥaṭṭōʾt hāʾāḏām), acting in breach of faith against YHWH (limaʿōl maʿal ḇaYHWH). The verb מָעַל (māʿal) — to trespass, act unfaithfully — is the key term for covenantal breach throughout the Torah (Lev 5:15; 26:40; Num 31:16; Deut 32:51). Sinning against a fellow person is simultaneously a trespass against YHWH — the covenant community's solidarity means harm to any member is harm to the covenant itself.
7: The remedial sequence: (1) hithwaddâ ʾet-ḥaṭṭāʾtô ʾăšer ʿāśâ — confess the sin; (2) full restitution (wəhēšîḇ ʾet-ʾăšāmô bərōʾšô); (3) add one-fifth (wǝḥămîšîtô yôsēp̄ ʿālāyw). This three-step process mirrors the guilt offering law of Lev 5:24 (Eng. 6:5) almost word-for-word; ch5 functions as the case law applying that sacrificial principle to the social realm. Confession before restitution establishes that the remedy is not merely financial but relational-covenantal.
8: If the wronged person has no kinsman-redeemer (gōʾēl) to receive restitution — because they are dead with no heir — the restitution goes to YHWH via the priest, plus the ram of atonement (ʾêl hakkippurîm). The scenario protects against unresolved obligations: if the wronged party's line has died out, the debt is not cancelled but transferred to the priestly system. No one can profit from a fellow person's extinction of family line.
9: Every gift of holy things (kol-tərûmâ lekol-qoḏšê bənê yiśrāʾēl) brought to the priest belongs to the priest. This verse interrupts the restitution law to clarify the priestly ownership of holy contributions, possibly introduced because v8 introduced the scenario of restitution going to the priest. The principle of priestly ownership of sacred contributions grounds the v8 provision in the broader system of priestly income.
10: wəʾîš ʾet-qoḏāšāyw lô yihyeh — each person's sacred gifts belong to that person. Whatever one consecrates remains one's own responsibility until it is properly transferred to the priest. The verse clarifies that voluntary gifts are not automatically the priest's property but become so only through proper offering — protecting the worshiper from arbitrary priestly claims while ensuring the priest receives what is duly presented.
11: The third and most extensive law in ch5 (vv11-31): the ordeal of bitter water for the suspected unfaithful wife (sotah in later Jewish tradition, from śātâ, to go astray). This is one of the most unusual legal texts in the Torah — a formally administered divine ordeal operating within the sanctuary by priestly mediation. The law occupies 21 verses, more than the two previous laws combined, signaling its complexity and contested character.
12: The triggering scenario: a man's wife has gone astray (tiśṭeh ʾiššô) and acted unfaithfully against him (māʿălâ bô māʿal). The verb māʿal — covenant trespass — applies here to the marital covenant as a subset of the covenant with YHWH. The accusation is about suspected rather than witnessed adultery; v13 immediately specifies that the act must be secret and unwitnessed, placing the entire case in the realm of suspected but unverifiable sin.
13: The critical condition: the act is hidden from her husband's eyes (wənistar meʿênê ʾîšāh), unwitnessed (wəhîʾ lōʾ nit̊p̄āśāh), and she is not caught. Without witnesses, no criminal charge can proceed in the Israelite legal system (Deut 19:15: two or three witnesses required). The ordeal procedure exists precisely for this evidentiary gap: suspected but unprovable. It does not apply to cases with witnesses — those are adjudicated normally.
14: The husband is overcome by a rûaḥ qinʾâ — a spirit of jealousy/suspicion. קִנְאָה (qinʾâ) — jealousy, zeal — is used for both divine and human intense relational possessiveness (Exod 20:5; Num 25:11; Prov 6:34). Two scenarios are covered: he is jealous because she actually defiled herself, or he is jealous despite her purity. The law provides for both, with different outcomes.
15: The husband brings his wife to the priest with a barley grain offering — the simplest and cheapest grain offering, associated with poverty offerings (Lev 5:11) and humiliation (lōʾ-yiṣōq ʿālāyw šemen wəlōʾ yittēn ʿālāyw ləḇōnâ — no oil, no frankincense). The humble offering reflects the ambiguous shame of the proceeding: neither party is yet convicted, but the situation is already one of humiliation for both.
16: The priest presents the woman before YHWH — the proceedings are formally covenantal: this is not a human judgment but a divine ordeal. The presentation before YHWH frames the entire procedure as a divine-court proceeding, with YHWH as the effective judge of what cannot be determined by human witnesses.
17: The priest prepares holy water (mayim qəḏōšîm) in a clay vessel and adds dust from the tabernacle floor (ʿāpār ʾăšer yihyeh bəqarqaʿ hammiškān). The water is not chemically special — it is ceremonially charged through the tabernacle connection. The dust from the sacred floor brings the sanctity of the divine dwelling into the liquid; the mixture is a physical compound of the holy space where YHWH dwells. The clay vessel (as opposed to a gold or silver vessel) keeps the mixture outside the highest-status material categories.
18: The priest uncovers the woman's head (ûp̄āraʿ ʾet-rōʾš hāʾiššâ) — the removal of the head covering signifies either mourning/humiliation or the stripping of normal social protection, placing her in a state of complete vulnerability before the divine judgment. She holds the grain offering of jealousy while the priest holds the bitter water. The literary arrangement — her hands full of the offering while the priest holds the water — stages her between the two instruments of judgment.
19: The oath of innocence: if no man has lain with her (ʾim-lōʾ šāḵaḇ ʾîš ʾôtāk) and she has not gone astray (wəʾim lōʾ śāṭît ṭumʾâ), she will be free from the bitter cursing water. The conditional formula (ʾim... ʾim) parallels ancient Near Eastern oath structures; the woman speaks her own testimony under sacred oath. Her speech-act becomes the instrument of her own judgment.
20: The positive scenario of the oath: if you have gone astray and defiled yourself while under your husband's authority (taḥat ʾîšek), if a man other than your husband has had sexual relations with you (ûttēn ʾet-šiḵbātəkā lāʾîš zûlāt ʾîšek). The explicit language is functional not salacious: the law must define the precise act that triggers the curse. The phrase taḥat ʾîšek — under your husband — frames adultery as a violation of the covenantal subordination structure of marriage.
21: The priestly curse invocation: yittēn YHWH ʾôtāk ləʾālâ wəliśḇûʿâ bətôk ʿammēk — may YHWH make you a curse and an oath among your people. The formula invokes YHWH as the agent of any punishment that follows; the water is the instrument, YHWH is the cause. The anatomical consequence — swollen belly and falling thigh (ṣəḇôt beten wənāp̄el yārēk) — is debated: may refer to uterine disease, miscarriage, prolapsed uterus, or symbolic infertility. The physical consequence is specifically reproductive, matching the nature of the suspected crime.
22: wəbāʾû hammayyim hamməʾarərîm hāʾēlleh bəmeʿayiḵ — may this water that brings the curse enter your body. The curse is invoked over the water itself, not merely spoken to the woman; the water becomes the carrier of covenantal judgment. The woman responds ʾāmēn ʾāmēn — the double affirmation of binding agreement throughout later liturgical tradition (Deut 27:15-26; Neh 8:6; 1 Cor 14:16) — making herself a co-participant in the oath rather than merely a passive subject of it.
23: The priest writes the curses in a scroll (səp̄er) and washes them into the bitter water. The written word physically dissolved into the water makes the verbal curse materially present in the liquid the woman will drink. This is sympathetic inscription — the word acts through its material carrier. The practice has ANE parallels in incantation bowls and curse tablets, but here it operates within the priestly system with YHWH as the operative agent rather than a magical formula.
24: wəhišqâ ʾet-hāʾiššâ ʾet-mê hammerār hamməʾarərîm — he shall make the woman drink the bitter water that brings the curse. Hammerār — bitter — characterizes the water experientially (the taste) and theologically (the cursing character). The sequence — curse written, washed into water, drunk — internalizes the divine judgment literally. The curse takes up residence in her body, and YHWH judges from within.
25: The priest then takes the grain offering of jealousy from the woman's hands, waves it (wəhēnîp̄) before YHWH, and brings it to the altar. The grain offering's wave-and-altar sequence follows the standard fellowship-offering procedure (Lev 7:30-31), integrating the sotah proceeding into the regular sacrificial system. The offering acknowledges YHWH's role as judge even as the water-ordeal enacts his judgment.
26: The priest burns a memorial portion (qomdāpṭô — the customary handful) on the altar, then gives the woman the water to drink. The offering at the altar and the drinking of the water are sequential: first the communal acknowledgment of divine presence (the altar), then the individual encounter with divine judgment (the water). The sequence keeps the ordeal within the framework of regular worship.
27: If she is guilty, the cursing water will cause the physical consequences described in v21 — the exact fulfillment of what she agreed to in v22. The text is matter-of-fact about divine action: YHWH does the judging, the water is his instrument. The Mishnah (Sotah 9:9) reports that Yochanan ben Zakkai abolished the sotah ordeal after 70 CE, arguing that when husbands were themselves not faithful, the waters could not function as an ordeal of the wife's faithfulness alone.
28: If innocent, wəniqqətâ wənizerəʿâ zāraʿ — she shall be free and shall conceive children. The positive outcome for the innocent woman is fertility rather than mere acquittal. The ordeal does not merely clear her name; it actively blesses her with the covenant blessing of offspring. This is an extraordinary legal guarantee: an innocent woman who has been falsely suspected and subjected to the humiliating ordeal is not merely vindicated but blessed. The law protects her against the social damage of accusation.
29: The legal summary: zōʾt tôrat haqqənāʾôt — this is the law of jealousy. The closing rubric mirrors the summaries of the purity laws above and anticipates the Nazirite law summary in 6:21. The word tôrâ here marks the jealousy-ordeal as a formal legal category with its own designated procedure.
30: The law covers both scenarios: a man whose wife has actually gone astray, and a man overcome by jealousy when his wife has not strayed. The symmetry of coverage — guilty and innocent alike go through the same procedure — establishes that the purpose of the law is to produce divine determination, not to assume guilt. The law is designed for epistemic situations where human evidence is absent, not for punishment of known guilt.
31: wəniqqa hāʾîš mēʿāwōn wəhāʾiššâ hahîʾ tiśśāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōnāh — the man shall be free of guilt, and the woman shall bear her own iniquity. The asymmetry here — the husband's guilt is removed regardless, the wife bears her iniquity if guilty — has drawn significant critical attention. The law exempts the husband from any parallel ordeal for his own potential unfaithfulness. The ANE context and the patriarchal household structure of ancient Israel explain the asymmetry historically, even as later tradition (and the NT's ethic of mutual fidelity) reads beyond it. What the law does guarantee is that the guilty wife is judged by YHWH alone — no human punishment is authorized unless guilt is supernaturally confirmed.
